en.extract:
The Javan scimitar babbler (Pomatorhinus montanus) is a species of bird in the family Timaliidae. It is endemic to Java, Indonesia. The Sunda scimitar babbler (P. bornensis), which is found in Sumatra, Borneo, and Malaysia, was formerly considered conspecific, with both species being grouped as the chestnut-backed scimitar babbler. Its natural habitats are subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest and subtropical or tropical moist montane forest.
It feeds on invertebrates.


References

Collar, N. J. & Robson, C. 2007. Family Timaliidae (Babblers)  pp. 70 – 291 in; del Hoyo, J., Elliott, A. & Christie, D.A. eds. Handbook of the Birds of the World, Vol. 12. Picathartes to Tits and Chickadees. Lynx Edicions, Barcelona.

The '''Javan scimitar babbler''' ('''''Pomatorhinus montanus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Timaliidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Java]], [[Indonesia]]. The [[Sunda scimitar babbler]] (''P. bornensis''), which is found in [[Sumatra]], [[Borneo]], and [[Malaysia]], was formerly considered conspecific, with both species being grouped as the '''chestnut-backed scimitar babbler'''.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Species Updates – IOC World Bird List|url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/updates/species-updates/|access-date=2021-05-28|language=en-US}}</ref> Its natural [[habitat]]s are [[subtropical or tropical moist lowland forest]] and subtropical or tropical moist [[montane forest]].

It feeds on invertebrates.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Simamora |first1=T.I. |last2=Purbowo |first2=S.D. |last3=Laumonier |first3=Y. |date=June 2021 |title=Looking for indicator bird species in the context of forest fragmentation and isolation in West Kalimantan, Indonesia |journal=Global Ecology and Conservation |volume=27 |article-number=e01610 |doi=10.1016/j.gecco.2021.e01610 |bibcode=2021GEcoC..2701610S |issn=2351-9894}}</ref>

zh.extract:
栗背钩嘴鹛（学名：Pomatorhinus montanus），是画眉科钩嘴鹛属的一种，分布于文莱、泰国、印度尼西亚和马来西亚。该物种的保护状况被评为无危。
栗背钩嘴鹛的平均体重约为28.7克。栖息地包括亚热带或热带的湿润低地林和亚热带或热带的湿润山地林。


参考文献

Pomatorhinus montanus. BirdLife.org.   [2011-06-27].
